title: Dark-pattern consent vs consent prompt vs cookie consent vs cookie banner vs legal acceptance vs preference center
summary: Flag dark-pattern consent when a consent or tracking choice is shaped to obtain agreement through hidden refusal, unequal effort, preselection, bundling, pressure, obstruction, or processing before choice; use consent prompt for the corrected optional data-use opt-in, cookie consent for purpose-level cookie runtime enforcement, cookie banner for the first-layer cookie surface, legal acceptance for required terms, and preference center for later durable settings.
decisionRules: Choose dark-pattern consent when Accept all is one click but Reject all, customize, or withdraw is hidden, delayed, visually de-emphasized, split across screens, or labelled to shame refusal.

Choose dark-pattern consent when optional marketing, analytics, research, AI training, partner sharing, personalization, or advertising purposes are preselected or bundled into a broad agree and continue action.
Choose dark-pattern consent when optional cookies, pixels, tags, SDKs, sharing, marketing, or AI training starts before the user makes the matching choice.
Choose dark-pattern consent when refusal blocks eligible service access, triggers repeated nags, erases task progress, or makes withdrawal materially harder than acceptance.
Choose consent prompt when the corrected task is a specific optional non-cookie data-use opt-in with named requester, data, purpose, optionality, accept, decline, withdrawal, and evidence record.
Choose cookie consent when the corrected task is purpose-level runtime control for cookies, pixels, local storage, tags, SDKs, browser signals, withdrawal, renewal, and tag blocking.
Choose cookie banner when the immediate design surface is the first-layer notice that introduces cookie choices and routes to accept, reject, or manage settings.
Choose legal acceptance when the user must accept required terms, conditions, contracts, policies, or disclosures; optional consent must stay separate from the legal agreement.
Choose preference center when users return later to manage consent, cookies, communications, language, privacy, and account preferences from a durable hub.

Do not count a consent record as valid evidence if the visible path relied on preselected choices, continued browsing, inactivity, hidden refusal, deceptive contrast, bundled purposes, consent wall pressure, or processing before choice.
failureModes: The first layer has Accept all and Manage settings, but Reject all exists only after several screens.
Optional analytics, advertising, personalization, and partner sharing are already selected when the user opens settings.
A checkout terms checkbox also opts users into marketing emails and partner sharing.
A site fires advertising tags before the cookie banner is answered.
A user who rejects optional tracking is blocked from ordinary service content or repeatedly prompted until accepting.
Withdrawal exists only in a privacy policy paragraph rather than the active preference or consent surface.

sources.note: Supports freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous consent, clear affirmative action, genuine choice, granular purposes, no pre-ticked boxes, and no inactivity-based consent.
Supports consent recording, management, withdrawal, refresh, and evidence requirements.
Supports granular consent, clear affirmative action, no silence or pre-ticked boxes, and withdrawal as easy as giving consent.
Supports reject-option, pre-ticked-box, deceptive-link, contrast, essential-cookie, and withdrawal concerns.
Supports no tracking before consent, no scroll or swipe consent, continued access after refusal, proof of consent, and easy withdrawal.
Supports clear and conspicuous material disclosures with appropriate proximity and prominence.
